questions.q: Which collection on a set $X$ is always a $\sigma$-algebra?
choices: All finite subsets of an infinite $X$
questions.explain: $\{\varnothing, X\}$ is closed under complements and countable unions. Open sets and intervals are not closed under complements, and finite subsets are not closed under countable unions in an infinite set.

questions.q: Let $\mu$ be a measure with $\mu(A)=3$, $\mu(B)=4$, and $\mu(A\cap B)=1$. Compute $\mu(A\cup B)$.
questions.explain: Use finite additivity with overlap correction: $\mu(A\cup B)=\mu(A)+\mu(B)-\mu(A\cap B)=3+4-1=6$.

questions.q: Write $e^{i\pi}$ as $a+bi$.
questions.explain: Euler's formula gives $e^{i\theta}=\cos\theta+i\sin\theta$, so $e^{i\pi}=\cos\pi+i\sin\pi=-1+0i$.

hard.q: Let $\mathcal{A}$ be the collection of all subsets of $\mathbb{N}$ that are either finite or have finite complement. Which property fails, showing $\mathcal{A}$ is NOT a $\sigma$-algebra?
hard.explain: Take $A_n=\{2n\}$ for $n\ge 1$; each $A_n$ is finite so $A_n\in\mathcal{A}$. But $\bigcup_{n=1}^\infty A_n$ is the set of all even positive integers, which is infinite and has infinite complement (the odd naturals), so it is not in $\mathcal{A}$. Thus $\mathcal{A}$ fails closure under countable unions.

hard.q: Let $(X,\mathcal{A},\mu)$ be a measure space with $\mu(X)=1$. Suppose $A_1\supset A_2\supset\cdots$ with $\mu(A_1)=0.8$ and $\mu(A_n)\to L$. If $\mu(A_3)=0.5$ and $\mu(A_5)=0.3$, and $\bigcap_{n=1}^\infty A_n=\varnothing$, what is $L$?
hard.explain: By continuity of measure from above: if $\mu(A_1)<\infty$ and $A_n\downarrow\varnothing$, then $\mu(A_n)\to\mu(\varnothing)=0$. The values at $n=3,5$ are irrelevant to the limit.

hard.q: Which is the correct order of steps in proving that the Borel $\sigma$-algebra $\mathcal{B}(\mathbb{R})$ contains all closed sets?
choices: Note closed sets are complements of open sets; since $\mathcal{B}(\mathbb{R})$ contains all open sets and is closed under complements, it contains all closed sets
Construct closed sets from Borel sets using countable intersections only
Use the fact that every closed set is a countable union of open intervals
Apply the monotone class theorem directly
hard.explain: $\mathcal{B}(\mathbb{R})$ is defined as the smallest $\sigma$-algebra containing all open sets. Since every closed set $F=U^c$ for some open $U$, and $\sigma$-algebras are closed under complementation, $F\in\mathcal{B}(\mathbb{R})$.

questions.q: Which property is guaranteed for the Lebesgue outer measure $m^*$ on all subsets of $\mathbb{R}$, even on non-measurable sets?
choices: Translation invariance only for Borel sets
questions.explain: Outer measure is countably subadditive on all of $\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{R})$ by construction (take covers of each $E_n$ and concatenate). Countable additivity fails on non-measurable sets — that is precisely what Carathéodory's criterion diagnoses.

questions.q: A set $E\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ is Carathéodory-measurable when, for every test set $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$:
questions.explain: Carathéodory's additivity criterion: $E$ splits every test set additively against the outer measure. The $\le$ direction is free by subadditivity; the $\ge$ direction is the substantive requirement.

questions.q: Compute the Lebesgue outer measure $m^*(\mathbb{Q}\cap[0,1])$.
questions.explain: Enumerate $\mathbb{Q}\cap[0,1]=\{q_k\}$; cover $q_k$ by $(q_k-\varepsilon/2^{k+1},q_k+\varepsilon/2^{k+1})$. Total cover length is $\varepsilon$, and $\varepsilon>0$ was arbitrary, so $m^*=0$.

hard.q: Which statement about Carathéodory's extension theorem is correct?
choices: Every outer measure extends uniquely to a measure on $\mathcal{P}(X)$
A premeasure $\mu_0$ on an algebra $\mathcal{A}_0$ extends to a measure on $\sigma(\mathcal{A}_0)$, uniquely if $\mu_0$ is $\sigma$-finite
The extension is automatic with no hypothesis on $\mu_0$
Uniqueness holds without any finiteness assumption
hard.hint: Existence is unconditional; the subtle hypothesis is what guarantees uniqueness.
hard.explain: Carathéodory: every premeasure extends to an outer measure, and the measurable sets form a $\sigma$-algebra on which the outer measure is countably additive. Existence is free; $\sigma$-finiteness is the clean hypothesis that makes the extension to $\sigma(\mathcal{A}_0)$ unique.

hard.q: Why can't countable additivity hold for $m^*$ on all of $\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{R})$?
choices: Because $\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{R})$ is too large as a set
Because Vitali's non-measurable set (using AC) has translates that would force $m^*([0,1])\in\{0,\infty\}$
Because every subset of $\mathbb{R}$ has positive outer measure
hard.explain: Vitali's construction (partition $[0,1]$ by the $\mathbb{Q}$-translation equivalence and pick one representative per class) produces a set $V$ whose $\mathbb{Q}\cap[-1,1]$-translates are disjoint and cover a finite-length interval. Translation invariance + countable additivity + non-zero $m^*(V)$ force contradictions on both sides (either 0 or $\infty$).

hard.q: Let $\mu_0$ be the length premeasure on the algebra of finite unions of half-open intervals $(a,b]\subset\mathbb{R}$. After Carathéodory extension, what is the Lebesgue outer measure of the fat Cantor set obtained by removing middle intervals of length $4^{-n}$ from $[0,1]$ at stage $n$?
hard.explain: Total removed length is $\sum_{n=1}^\infty 2^{n-1}\cdot 4^{-n}=\sum_{n=1}^\infty 2^{-n-1}=1/2$. The remaining closed set has Lebesgue measure $1-1/2=1/2$ — a Lebesgue-positive-measure set with empty interior, showing Carathéodory measurability is strictly subtler than topology.

questions.q: What is the Lebesgue measure of a singleton $\{x\}\subset\mathbb{R}$?
questions.explain: Points have zero length, so every singleton has Lebesgue measure $0$.

questions.q: Compute $m([2,5])$, where $m$ is Lebesgue measure on $\mathbb{R}$.
questions.explain: Intervals are measured by length: $m([a,b])=b-a$, so $m([2,5])=5-2=3$.

questions.q: Compute $(1+i)^2$.
questions.explain: $(1+i)^2=1+2i+i^2=2i$, so the real part is $0$ and imaginary part is $2$.

hard.q: The Cantor set $C$ is constructed by removing middle thirds from $[0,1]$. Which statement correctly describes its Lebesgue measure and cardinality?
hard.explain: At stage $n$, total removed length is $\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}2^k/3^{k+1}\to 1$, so $m(C)=0$. Yet $C$ is in bijection with $\{0,1\}^{\mathbb{N}}$ via ternary expansions, making it uncountable. This is the standard counterexample of an uncountable null set.

hard.q: A Vitali set $V\subset[0,1]$ is non-measurable. Assuming translation-invariance, if $\{V+q:q\in\mathbb{Q}\cap[-1,1]\}$ are pairwise disjoint and cover $[0,2]$, what value does $\sum_{q}m(V+q)$ take if $m(V)$ existed and were positive?
hard.explain: If $m(V)=c>0$, countable additivity gives $m([0,2])\ge\sum_{q}c=\infty$, contradicting $m([0,2])=2$. If $m(V)=0$, then $m([0,2])=0$, again a contradiction. So no consistent value of $m(V)$ exists; the correct answer reflecting the only non-contradictory finite value attempted is $0$, but in truth measurability fails entirely.

hard.q: Which is the correct proof-step order for showing outer regularity of Lebesgue measure: $m(E)=\inf\{m(U):U\supset E,\ U\text{ open}\}$?
choices: Cover $E$ by open intervals; sum their lengths; take infimum; verify the infimum is attained by an open set
Use inner regularity first; then dualize
Apply Hahn decomposition and restrict to the positive part
hard.explain: For any $\varepsilon>0$, cover $E$ by open intervals $I_n$ with $\sum m(I_n)<m(E)+\varepsilon$; their union $U=\bigcup I_n$ is open, contains $E$, and $m(U)\le\sum m(I_n)<m(E)+\varepsilon$. Since $m(U)\ge m(E)$ always, the infimum equals $m(E)$.

questions.q: A function $f\colon(X,\mathcal A)\to(\mathbb R,\mathcal B)$ is measurable iff for every Borel set $B\subset\mathbb R$ we have:
questions.explain: Measurability is defined by preimages: all Borel sets pull back to measurable sets in $\mathcal A$.

questions.q: Let $f=\mathbf 1_{[0,2]}$ on $\mathbb R$. What is $\int f\,dm$?
questions.explain: The indicator integral equals measure of the set: $\int \mathbf 1_E\,dm = m(E)$, so $m([0,2])=2$.

questions.q: Compute $\overline{(3-4i)}$.
questions.explain: Complex conjugation flips the sign of the imaginary part: $\overline{a+bi}=a-bi$.

hard.q: Let $f_n\to f$ pointwise. Which is the correct counterexample showing that pointwise limits of measurable functions can be non-measurable if the $\sigma$-algebra is not complete?
choices: Take $f_n=f$ constant; the limit is obviously measurable
Take a non-measurable set $N$ inside a null set $Z$; set $f=\mathbf{1}_N$; approximate by $f_n=0$; then $f_n\to f$ pointwise but $f$ is non-measurable in the incomplete $\sigma$-algebra
Pointwise limits of measurable functions are always measurable by the Borel $\sigma$-algebra axioms
Use dominated convergence to conclude measurability
hard.explain: In an incomplete measure space, a null set $Z$ may contain non-measurable subsets $N$. Then $f_n=0$ (measurable) converges pointwise to $\mathbf{1}_N$, which is not measurable. Completeness is exactly the condition that prevents this: it ensures every subset of a null set is measurable.

hard.q: Let $f:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}$ be a simple function $f=3\cdot\mathbf{1}_A+7\cdot\mathbf{1}_B$ where $A=[0,1]$, $B=(2,4]$, and $A\cap B=\varnothing$. Compute $\int f\,dm$.
hard.explain: For a simple function $\int\sum c_i\mathbf{1}_{E_i}\,dm=\sum c_i m(E_i)$. So $\int f\,dm=3\cdot m([0,1])+7\cdot m((2,4])=3\cdot 1+7\cdot 2=3+14=17$.

hard.q: Which is the correct proof-step ordering for showing that $f+g$ is measurable when $f,g:(X,\mathcal{A})\to\mathbb{R}$ are measurable?
choices: Write $\{f+g>a\}=\bigcup_{q\in\mathbb{Q}}(\{f>q\}\cap\{g>a-q\})$; each set is in $\mathcal{A}$; countable union stays in $\mathcal{A}$
Use the product $\sigma$-algebra on $\mathbb{R}^2$; map $(f,g)$; compose with addition
Apply Lusin's theorem to approximate by continuous functions
Note addition is continuous so measurability is automatic
hard.explain: The standard elementary proof: $f+g>a$ iff $f>a-g$ iff $\exists q\in\mathbb{Q}$ with $f>q>a-g$, giving $\{f+g>a\}=\bigcup_{q\in\mathbb{Q}}(\{f>q\}\cap\{g>a-q\})$. Each factor is measurable; $\mathcal{A}$ is closed under finite intersections and countable unions.

questions.q: For nonnegative measurable $f$, the Lebesgue integral is defined as:
choices: an infimum over upper Riemann sums
a supremum of integrals of simple functions $s\le f$
the limit of midpoint sums only
questions.explain: For $f\ge 0$, $\int f\,d\mu := \sup\{\int s\,d\mu : 0\le s\le f,\ s\text{ simple}\}$.

questions.q: Compute $\int_0^1 x\,dx$ (Lebesgue measure).
questions.explain: Lebesgue and Riemann integrals agree here: $\int_0^1 x\,dx = [x^2/2]_0^1 = 1/2$.

questions.q: Compute $\int_0^{2\pi} e^{ix}\,dx$ and report as $a+bi$.
questions.explain: $e^{ix}=\cos x+i\sin x$. Both $\int_0^{2\pi}\cos x\,dx$ and $\int_0^{2\pi}\sin x\,dx$ are $0$, so the complex integral is $0+0i$.

hard.q: Which function is Lebesgue integrable on $[0,1]$ but NOT Riemann integrable?
hard.explain: The Dirichlet function is nowhere continuous, so it is not Riemann integrable (upper and lower Riemann sums differ). However, $\mathbb{Q}\cap[0,1]$ is countable hence has Lebesgue measure $0$, so $\int_0^1\mathbf{1}_{\mathbb{Q}}\,dm=0$. It is Lebesgue integrable.

hard.q: Compute $\int_1^e \ln(x)\,dx$ using the Lebesgue integral (equal to the Riemann integral here).
hard.explain: Integration by parts: $\int_1^e \ln x\,dx=[x\ln x - x]_1^e=(e\cdot 1-e)-(1\cdot 0-1)=0+1=1$.

hard.q: Let $f_n(x)=n\cdot\mathbf{1}_{(0,1/n]}$. Then $f_n\to 0$ pointwise a.e. yet $\int f_n\,dm=1$ for all $n$. Which hypothesis of the Dominated Convergence Theorem fails?
choices: Existence of an integrable dominator $g\ge|f_n|$ for all $n$
hard.explain: Any dominator must satisfy $g(x)\ge n\cdot\mathbf{1}_{(0,1/n]}(x)$ for all $n$, which forces $g(x)\ge 1/x$ near $0$. But $\int_0^1 1/x\,dm=\infty$, so no integrable dominator exists. DCT requires $|f_n|\le g$ for some $g\in L^1$, which fails here.

questions.q: If $0\le f_n\uparrow f$ pointwise, which theorem gives $\int f_n\to\int f$?
questions.explain: MCT exactly states: for increasing nonnegative measurable $f_n$, integrals converge upward to the integral of the limit.

questions.q: Let $f_n(x)=x^n$ on $[0,1]$. Compute $\int_0^1 f_n(x)\,dx$ in terms of $n$ at $n=9$.
questions.explain: $\int_0^1 x^n\,dx = 1/(n+1)$, so for $n=9$ the value is $1/10=0.1$.

questions.q: Compute $i^5$ as $a+bi$.
questions.explain: Powers of $i$ cycle every $4$: $i^5=i^{4+1}=i$.

hard.q: Fatou's lemma states $\int\liminf_n f_n\,d\mu\le\liminf_n\int f_n\,d\mu$ for $f_n\ge 0$. Which sequence shows the inequality can be strict?
hard.hint: Look for fixed-mass bumps whose support slides off toward infinity.
hard.explain: $f_n=\mathbf{1}_{[n,n+1]}\to 0$ pointwise, so $\liminf f_n=0$ and $\int\liminf f_n=0$. But $\int f_n=1$ for all $n$, so $\liminf_n\int f_n=1>0$. Strict inequality holds because mass escapes to infinity.

hard.q: Let $g_n(x)=\mathbf{1}_{[0,n]}(x)\cdot e^{-x}$ on $[0,\infty)$. By MCT (the sequence is increasing and nonneg), $\lim_{n\to\infty}\int_0^\infty g_n\,dm$ equals what?
hard.explain: $g_n\uparrow e^{-x}$ pointwise, so MCT gives $\lim\int g_n=\int_0^\infty e^{-x}\,dm=[-e^{-x}]_0^\infty=0-(-1)=1$.

hard.q: Which is the correct proof-step order for the Monotone Convergence Theorem (for $0\le f_n\uparrow f$)?
choices: Show $\limsup\int f_n\le\int f$ using simple-function approximations; show $\int f\le\liminf\int f_n$ by Fatou; conclude equality

Show $\int f\le\liminf\int f_n$ by monotonicity; show $\limsup\int f_n\le\int f$ by fixing a simple $0\le s\le f$ and $c\in(0,1)$, applying measure of $\{f_n\ge cs\}\uparrow X$; conclude $\int f=\lim\int f_n$
Use the Radon–Nikodym theorem to pass the limit inside
hard.explain: Standard MCT proof: monotonicity gives $\int f_n\le\int f$, so $\limsup\int f_n\le\int f$. For the reverse, fix simple $0\le s\le f$ and $c\in(0,1)$; the sets $E_n=\{f_n\ge cs\}$ increase to $X$; thus $\int f_n\ge c\int_{E_n}s\,d\mu\to c\int s\,d\mu$; let $c\to 1$ and take sup over $s$ to get $\liminf\int f_n\ge\int f$.

questions.q: Which statement is true on a finite-measure space?
questions.explain: By Hölder, on finite measure spaces one has $\|f\|_1\le \mu(X)^{1/2}\|f\|_2$, so $L^2\subset L^1$.

questions.q: On $[0,1]$, let $f(x)=x$. Compute $\|f\|_2$.
Compute $\left|\,1-i\,\right|$.

hard.q: On $[1,\infty)$ with Lebesgue measure, $f(x)=x^{-\alpha}$. For which values of $\alpha$ does $f\in L^2[1,\infty)$?
hard.explain: $\int_1^\infty x^{-2\alpha}\,dx$ converges iff $2\alpha>1$, i.e., $\alpha>1/2$. This is strictly weaker than $L^1$ membership (which requires $\alpha>1$), illustrating that $L^1\not\subset L^2$ on infinite-measure spaces.

hard.q: Let $f,g\in L^2([0,1])$ with $\|f\|_2=3$ and $\|g\|_2=4$. By the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality, $|\langle f,g\rangle|\le$ what value?
hard.explain: Cauchy–Schwarz: $|\langle f,g\rangle|\le\|f\|_2\|g\|_2=3\cdot 4=12$.

hard.q: Which is the correct counterexample showing $L^1([0,1])\not\subset L^2([0,1])$?
choices: $f(x)=x^{-2}$: not in $L^1$ either, so not a valid counterexample
hard.explain: $f(x)=x^{-1/2}$ satisfies $\int_0^1 x^{-1/2}\,dx=2$, so $f\in L^1$. But $\int_0^1 x^{-1}\,dx=\infty$, so $f\notin L^2$. This demonstrates that on $[0,1]$ (finite measure) the inclusion $L^2\subset L^1$ holds but the reverse fails.

questions.q: The product $\sigma$-algebra $\mathcal{A}\otimes\mathcal{B}$ on $X\times Y$ is:
choices: The collection of all subsets $A\times B$ with $A\in\mathcal{A}$, $B\in\mathcal{B}$
The $\sigma$-algebra generated by the measurable rectangles $A\times B$
Always equal to $\mathcal{B}(X\times Y)$ when $X,Y$ are topological spaces
questions.explain: $\mathcal{A}\otimes\mathcal{B}:=\sigma(\{A\times B:A\in\mathcal{A},B\in\mathcal{B}\})$. Measurable rectangles alone aren't closed under countable operations, hence the generation step.

questions.q: On $[0,1]\times[0,1]$ with Lebesgue $\otimes$ Lebesgue, compute $(m\otimes m)([0,0.5]\times[0,0.4])$.
questions.explain: $(m\otimes m)(A\times B)=m(A)\cdot m(B)=0.5\cdot 0.4=0.2$.

questions.q: A measure space $(X,\mathcal{A},\mu)$ is $\sigma$-finite when:
choices: $X$ is a countable union of measurable sets of finite measure
questions.explain: $\sigma$-finiteness says $X=\bigcup_n X_n$ with $\mu(X_n)<\infty$. Lebesgue measure on $\mathbb{R}$ is $\sigma$-finite ($\mathbb{R}=\bigcup_n[-n,n]$) but not finite.

hard.q: Which measure space fails to be $\sigma$-finite, so that product-measure uniqueness via Carathéodory can break?
choices: Counting measure on an uncountable set $X$
hard.hint: $\sigma$-finite means $X$ is a countable union of sets of finite measure.
hard.explain: Counting measure on an uncountable $X$ assigns $\infty$ to every infinite set and $n$ to each $n$-element finite set; a countable union of finite sets is still countable, so $X$ itself cannot be written that way. Uniqueness in the Carathéodory extension can then fail (two distinct measures agreeing on the algebra but disagreeing on the generated $\sigma$-algebra).

hard.q: On $(\mathbb{R}^2,\mathcal{B}\otimes\mathcal{B},m\otimes m)$, compute the measure of the triangle $\{(x,y):0\le y\le x\le 1\}$.
hard.explain: By Tonelli: $\int_0^1\int_0^x 1\,dy\,dx=\int_0^1 x\,dx=1/2$.

hard.q: Why does $\sigma$-finiteness of both $\mu$ and $\nu$ make $\mu\otimes\nu$ uniquely determined on $\mathcal{A}\otimes\mathcal{B}$?
choices: Because every product of two $\sigma$-finite measures is itself a probability measure
Because the algebra of finite disjoint unions of measurable rectangles is $\sigma$-finite under the premeasure $(\mu\otimes\nu)(A\times B):=\mu(A)\nu(B)$, and Carathéodory's uniqueness applies
Because product measures exist only when both factors are probability measures
hard.explain: Existence is via Carathéodory applied to the rectangle premeasure on the algebra of finite disjoint rectangle unions. Uniqueness in Carathéodory's extension theorem needs the premeasure to be $\sigma$-finite, which follows from $\sigma$-finiteness of both $\mu$ and $\nu$.

questions.q: Tonelli's theorem (for $\sigma$-finite $\mu,\nu$) swaps iterated integrals with no integrability hypothesis, provided:
questions.explain: Tonelli requires only nonnegativity + joint measurability; all three expressions in $[0,\infty]$ are equal unconditionally. Fubini is the companion statement for complex-valued $f\in L^1(\mu\otimes\nu)$.

questions.q: Compute $\int_0^1\!\int_0^1 xy\,dx\,dy$.
questions.explain: By Tonelli: $\int_0^1 y\,dy\cdot\int_0^1 x\,dx=(1/2)(1/2)=1/4$.

questions.q: The operational protocol before swapping iterated integrals of a signed function $f(x,y)$ is:
choices: Swap freely — Tonelli always applies to measurable functions
Compute $\int\int|f|$ via Tonelli first; if finite, invoke Fubini to swap
Use Radon–Nikodym to reduce to an absolutely continuous measure first
questions.explain: Standard workflow: Tonelli on $|f|$ gives an unambiguous $[0,\infty]$-valued iterated integral. If this is finite, $f\in L^1(\mu\otimes\nu)$ and Fubini licenses swapping. If it diverges, the iterated integrals of $f$ can disagree.

hard.q: On $X=Y=\mathbb{N}$ with counting measure, $a_{m,n}=1$ if $n=m$, $-1$ if $n=m+1$, else $0$. Which statement is correct?
choices: $\sum_m\sum_n a_{m,n}=0$ but $\sum_n\sum_m a_{m,n}=1$, so Fubini fails because $\sum_{m,n}|a_{m,n}|=\infty$
hard.hint: Sum by rows first, then by columns, and notice the telescoping mismatch.
hard.explain: Row sums: for each $m$, $a_{m,m}+a_{m,m+1}=1-1=0$, so $\sum_m 0=0$. Column sums: for $n=0$, only $a_{0,0}=1$ contributes (since $a_{m,0}\ne 0$ only at $m=0$), and for $n\ge 1$, $a_{n,n}+a_{n-1,n}=1-1=0$. Total $=1$. $|a_{m,n}|$ has $\sum=\infty$, so Fubini fails.

hard.q: Compute $\int_0^\infty\int_0^\infty e^{-xy}\sin x\,dx\,dy$ assuming absolute integrability allows the swap. (Hint: swap and use $\int_0^\infty e^{-xy}\sin x\,dy=\sin x/x$.)
hard.explain: Swap: $\int_0^\infty\sin x\int_0^\infty e^{-xy}\,dy\,dx=\int_0^\infty \frac{\sin x}{x}\,dx=\pi/2$. Absolute integrability in the stated form is delicate here, but the iterated computation yields the classical Dirichlet integral.

hard.q: Which is the correct proof-step ordering for Fubini (for $f\in L^1(\mu\otimes\nu)$, $\sigma$-finite case)?
choices: Prove for $\mathbf{1}_{A\times B}$; linearity to simple; MCT to $[0,\infty]$-valued (that is Tonelli); split $f=f^+-f^-$ and use integrability to subtract
Use DCT to interchange the order
Reduce to the Riemann integral via uniform continuity
hard.explain: Fubini is built up in layers: indicator rectangles → simple functions → nonneg measurable (Tonelli) → integrable by splitting $f^\pm$. The $\sigma$-finiteness hypothesis is used in uniqueness of the product measure and in applying Tonelli to $|f|$ to avoid $\infty-\infty$.

questions.q: The Jordan decomposition of a signed measure $\nu$ expresses $\nu$ as:
questions.explain:
Hahn gives a partition $X=P\sqcup N$ into a positive set and a negative set; $
u^+(E)=
u(E\cap P)$ and $
u^-(E)=-
u(E\cap N)$ are mutually singular positive measures summing to $|
u|=
u^++
u^-$, and $
u=
u^+-
u^-$.

questions.q: We say $\nu\ll\mu$ (absolutely continuous) when:
questions.explain:
$
u\ll\mu$ iff every $\mu$-null set is also $
u$-null. Radon–Nikodym gives a density $f=d
u/d\mu\ge 0$ with $
u(E)=\int_E f\,d\mu$ when $\mu$ is $\sigma$-finite and $
u\ll\mu$.

questions.q: On $[0,1]$ with Lebesgue $m$, let $d\nu=2x\,dm$. Compute $\nu([0,1])$.
questions.explain: $\nu([0,1])=\int_0^1 2x\,dm=x^2\big|_0^1=1$. The density $f(x)=2x$ is the Radon–Nikodym derivative $d\nu/dm$.

hard.q: Which is the correct statement of the Lebesgue decomposition theorem?
choices: Every $\sigma$-finite measure $\nu$ has a unique density with respect to $\mu$

For $\sigma$-finite $\mu,
u$, there exist unique $
u_{ac}\ll\mu$ and $
u_s\perp\mu$ with $
u=
u_{ac}+
u_s$
Every signed measure is absolutely continuous
Signed measures are always singular to Lebesgue measure
hard.explain:
Lebesgue decomposition: $
u=
u_{ac}+
u_s$ with $
u_{ac}\ll\mu$ (Radon–Nikodym applies) and $
u_s\perp\mu$ (concentrated on a $\mu$-null set). The two pieces are uniquely determined.

hard.q: On $[0,1]$ with Lebesgue $m$, let $d\nu=f\,dm$ with $f(x)=3x^2$. Compute $\nu([0,1/2])$.
Which measure on $[0,1]$ is NOT absolutely continuous with respect to Lebesgue measure $m$?
hard.explain: The Dirac mass $\delta_{1/2}$ concentrates all mass at the single point $\{1/2\}$, which has $m(\{1/2\})=0$. So $m(\{1/2\})=0$ but $\delta_{1/2}(\{1/2\})=1$, violating $\nu\ll m$. $\delta_{1/2}\perp m$ instead.

questions.q: The Riesz–Markov–Kakutani theorem says: every positive linear functional $\Lambda$ on $C_c(X)$ for locally compact Hausdorff $X$ is:
choices: A finite sum of Dirac masses
$\Lambda(f)=\int_X f\,d\mu$ for a unique regular Borel measure $\mu$
questions.explain: Riesz representation: positive linear functionals on $C_c(X)$ are exactly integration against regular Borel measures (inner regular on open sets, outer regular on Borel sets, finite on compact sets). The bijection is the bridge from functional analysis to measure theory.

questions.q: On $X=[0,1]$, let $\Lambda(f)=f(1/3)$ for $f\in C([0,1])$. What is $\mu([0,1/2])$ for the representing measure $\mu$?
Which regularity property of the representing measure is essential to Riesz representation?
choices: Inner regularity on open sets and outer regularity on Borel sets
Absolute continuity with respect to Lebesgue measure
questions.explain: The representing Radon/regular Borel measure $\mu$ satisfies: $\mu(U)=\sup\{\mu(K):K\subset U\text{ compact}\}$ on open $U$, and $\mu(E)=\inf\{\mu(U):E\subset U\text{ open}\}$ on Borel $E$. These conditions pin $\mu$ down uniquely.

hard.q: Which functional on $C_c(\mathbb{R})$ fails to be representable by a Radon measure?
hard.hint: One of these is not positive and not continuous on $C_c$ with the sup-norm topology.
hard.explain: $\Lambda(f)=f'(0)$ is not a positive functional: take $f\ge 0$ with a strict max at $0$, then $f'(0)=0$ or even $\le 0$, so positivity fails. It is a well-defined linear functional on $C^1_c$, but Riesz representation only covers positive linear functionals on $C_c$.

hard.q: On $X=[0,1]$, let $\Lambda(f)=\int_0^1 f(x)\cdot 2x\,dx$. What is $\mu([0,1])$ for the representing measure?
hard.explain: $\Lambda(f)=\int f\,d\mu$ where $d\mu=2x\,dm$. So $\mu([0,1])=\int_0^1 2x\,dm=1$.

hard.q: Which consequence of Riesz representation is used to build Haar measure on a locally compact group $G$?
choices: Existence of a translation-invariant positive linear functional on $C_c(G)$, which then gives a translation-invariant Radon measure
hard.explain: One shows that $C_c(G)$ admits a translation-invariant positive linear functional $\Lambda$ (existence by a Hahn–Banach / tangent-functional argument), then Riesz representation upgrades $\Lambda$ to a left-Haar measure. Uniqueness up to scaling is a separate argument.

questions.q: The $s$-dimensional Hausdorff outer measure $\mathcal{H}^s(E)$ is defined as:
questions.explain: $\mathcal{H}^s_\delta(E)=\inf\{\sum_i\mathrm{diam}(U_i)^s:E\subset\bigcup_i U_i,\ \mathrm{diam}(U_i)\le\delta\}$, and $\mathcal{H}^s(E)=\lim_{\delta\to 0}\mathcal{H}^s_\delta(E)$. This limit is monotone decreasing in $\delta$, hence well-defined in $[0,\infty]$.

questions.q: Compute the Hausdorff dimension of the middle-thirds Cantor set $C$. Report as a decimal to 3 places. (Answer: $\log 2/\log 3$.)
questions.explain: $C$ is self-similar with two similarities of ratio $1/3$. The Moran equation $2\cdot(1/3)^s=1$ gives $s=\log 2/\log 3\approx 0.6309$.

questions.q: The Hausdorff dimension of a set $E$ is:
choices: The unique $s$ at which $\mathcal{H}^s(E)$ jumps from $\infty$ to $0$
questions.explain: $s\mapsto\mathcal{H}^s(E)$ is $\infty$ for small $s$ and $0$ for large $s$, with a unique transition point $s^*=\dim_H(E)$. At $s^*$ itself, $\mathcal{H}^{s^*}(E)$ may take any value in $[0,\infty]$.

hard.q: Which Hausdorff-measure statement is correct for the $s$-dimensional Sierpiński gasket (three contractions of ratio $1/2$)?
hard.hint: Moran's equation $N\cdot r^s=1$ for $N$ self-similar copies of ratio $r$.
hard.explain: Moran: $3\cdot(1/2)^s=1\Rightarrow s=\log 3/\log 2$. The gasket satisfies the Open Set Condition, which implies $0<\mathcal{H}^s(\text{gasket})<\infty$ at $s=\dim_H$.

hard.q: For the 4-contraction Koch curve with similarity ratio $1/3$, compute the Hausdorff dimension to 3 decimals. (Moran: $4\cdot(1/3)^s=1$.)
hard.explain: $s=\log 4/\log 3\approx 1.2619$. The Koch curve has strictly larger Hausdorff dimension than its topological dimension ($1$), reflecting its fractal crinkliness.

hard.q: Why does $\mathcal{H}^1$ on $\mathbb{R}$ agree with Lebesgue measure $m$?
choices: Because both are translation-invariant Radon measures on $\mathbb{R}$ agreeing on intervals (an interval $[a,b]$ has $\mathcal{H}^1=b-a$); uniqueness of the Haar/Lebesgue measure forces equality
By Radon–Nikodym with an irrational density
hard.explain: $\mathcal{H}^1([a,b])=b-a$ because optimal covers by small intervals have diameter-sum approaching $b-a$. Both $\mathcal{H}^1$ and $m$ are translation-invariant, $\sigma$-finite, and agree on a generating ring of intervals, so Carathéodory uniqueness forces $\mathcal{H}^1=m$ on $\mathcal{B}(\mathbb{R})$.
